comments.author: F2Scout
comments.text: Korv Batt just delivered the kind of performance that makes academy directors sit up straighter. Pole by 0.017 seconds, then a lights-to-flag sprint win where he lost the lead mid-race and calmly took it back with a brave move into Turn 1. That's not rookie composure — that's a driver who believes he belongs. The backstory Al-Rashid weaves — karting in Southeast Asia, far from the European powerhouses, caught by Mercedes at a shootout — makes this win feel earned. Prema have been waiting for Batt to convert promise into results. Jeddah was the moment. The championship now has Porter on top (34 pts), Batt third (28), and Melon suddenly scoreless and on the back foot. The top ten are separated by 24 points. This is the tightest F2 title fight in years.

replies.author: BattSignal
replies.text: I've been waiting for this since his Prema debut. Batt has always had the smooth, intelligent racecraft — he just needed the car underneath him. That SQ3 lap was a symphony. Three purple sectors, 1:29.812. The pass on Porter to retake the lead was a statement: he's not just a qualifier, he's a racer. Mercedes Academy have five drivers in F2, and Batt just became the one to watch.

replies.author: AnonymousGrunt_Fan
replies.text: Grunt finished 19th and scoreless. That's back-to-back weekends without points. The Melbourne feature win feels like a distant memory. His qualifying pace is becoming a real issue — he can't keep starting from the back and expecting to carve through. The championship is slipping away.

replies.author: F2Scout
replies.text: Grunt's race pace is still there — his best laps were competitive. But in a championship this tight, you can't afford to give away Saturdays. He's now P5 in the standings, 9 points off the lead. The feature race is critical.

comments.author: PremierFan
comments.text: Corentin Premier's radio messages broke me. 'Why can't I get a working car?!' After taking pole in Bahrain, after looking like a genuine title contender, his car just... stopped. The DNF drops him out of the top five in the standings. His frustration was raw — helmet in hand, staring at the track. Al-Rashid captured the agony perfectly. The VAR car clearly has reliability issues; Frid also had a difficult weekend. Red Bull Academy will be asking serious questions.

replies.author: FridTracker
replies.text: Frid managed P6, which salvaged something for VAR. But Premier's DNF is the story. He was running in the top ten, looked set for solid points, and then the car died. Two races in a row with mechanical issues is a pattern. The team need to get on top of it before the feature race.

comments.author: MittensMania
comments.text: Tim Mittens locked up at Turn 1, dropped to last, and finished P14. After the Bahrain nightmare, this is another blow. His early-season consistency has evaporated. Hitech look lost — Rivington didn't feature at all. The Haas Academy driver needs a clean feature race desperately. Two weekends without points in a championship this tight is a disaster.

replies.author: CatmanCarlo
replies.text: Mittens' confidence looks shaken. The lock-up was uncharacteristic — he's usually so composed. Hitech need to put an arm around him and reset. The pace is there; we saw it in Melbourne. But F2 doesn't wait for anyone.

comments.author: ChaosReigns
comments.text: F2 Jeddah sprint summary: Batt wins his first race from pole, Porter takes the championship lead with P2, Asdelian and Callahan duel all race for third, Premier's car dies and he's heartbroken, Melon fails to score and loses the championship lead, Dubišar gets knocked out in SQ1, Mittens locks up and finishes P14, guest driver Kaka scores points on debut. The top ten in the championship are separated by 24 points. Three different winners in three rounds. This series is absolutely unhinged and I love it.

replies.author: VoiceOfTheFan
replies.text: BATT WON!! PORTER LEADS THE CHAMPIONSHIP!! PREMIER'S RADIO MADE ME CRY. F2 IS BETTER THAN F1 THIS YEAR.

comments.author: ThompsonTracker
comments.text: Alex Thompson's weekend is a riddle: P6 in SQ1, dead last in SQ2, then recovers to P9 in the sprint. His radio message — 'Where did I go wrong?' — is the question of the weekend. Trident couldn't explain the drop-off. The Mercedes junior has pace, but the inconsistency is maddening. A clean feature race would do wonders for his confidence.

replies.author: RoadCourseRealist
replies.text: Thompson's GT3 road course struggles are well documented. But he kept it clean in the sprint and scored a point. That's progress, even if it doesn't feel like it. The feature race is another chance to build.

comments.author: MelonMomentum
comments.text: Ryan Melon went from championship leader to P12 in qualifying and scoreless in the sprint. That's the cruelty of this championship. The Rodin car wasn't there this weekend, and Melon couldn't drag it into the points. Porter now leads by 3 points, Batt is charging, and Melon suddenly looks vulnerable. The feature race will test his resilience.

replies.author: HongnoiHope
replies.text: And Hongnoi didn't score either. Rodin have gone from a 1-2 in the standings to both drivers outside the points in one weekend. That's a team-wide issue, not just a driver problem. They need to find pace before the feature.

comments.author: DAMS_Duo
comments.text: Asdelian and Callahan's battle was the highlight of the midfield. Three attempts before Asdelian finally got past, and then he held on for P3. Callahan P4. Double points for DAMS, who now sit second in the constructors' with 45 points. The French Alpine protégé is growing up before our eyes — that patience he showed was learned the hard way after a 2025 season full of errors. DAMS are quietly building a title challenge.

replies.author: KodiakCallahanFan
replies.text: Callahan's consistency is underrated. P4 today, points in every round. He's not flashy, but he's always there. DAMS have two drivers who can fight at the front. That's a championship-winning combination.

comments.author: GuestDriverWatch
comments.text: Ricardo Kaka — a guest entry for Rodin — finished P7 on debut. That's seriously impressive for a driver making a rare F2 appearance in a car he's never raced. He kept it clean, avoided the chaos, and scored points. Rodin might have found a hidden gem, even if it's a one-off.

comments.author: ParkAndRide
comments.text: Daniel Park topped SQ2 and finished P8. That's a solid weekend for the Korean after a tough Bahrain. The Mercedes Academy is so deep — Park, Thompson, Batt, Price, Callahan — and Batt just threw down the gauntlet. Park needs to convert these consistent points into a podium soon or he'll get lost in the shuffle.

replies.author: StatsNerd_26
replies.text: Park now has 9 points, Batt 28. The gap is widening. Topping SQ2 shows the one-lap pace is there. He just needs to string a full weekend together.

comments.author: CamposDespair
comments.text: Marcos Olmedo P10 — another point, but it feels like a missed opportunity. Aizawa didn't score. Campos are still P10 in the constructors' with only 7 points. The Ferrari Academy junior is scraping points while Batt and Porter are fighting for wins. The gap between the top teams and the rest is growing.

replies.author: TifosiForever
replies.text: Olmedo is a Ferrari junior, and he's being outperformed by Mercedes and Red Bull prospects. After Cachinho's struggles in F1, the Ferrari pipeline desperately needs a star. Olmedo and Rivington aren't delivering yet.

comments.author: FlyingFinn53
comments.text: I know this is F2 but I can't help thinking about EETU! He would have LOVED this Jeddah circuit! Anyway, congratulations to Batt — a Mercedes junior winning is good for the sport! But nothing compares to the ICE COLD FINN! KIITOS EETU!! 🇫🇮

replies.author: F2Scout
replies.text: Respectfully, this is an F2 thread. Batt just won his first race. Let him have his moment without the F1 comparisons.

comments.author: MemeLordMike
comments.text: Premier's car died like my motivation on a Monday morning
